title: Charmed Life - Chapter 4 (Fragment 5)
text:
When the half hour was over, Mr. Saunders, without turning around, told Gwendolen to stop pouring ink and do sums. Then, still without turning around, he said to Cat, “Eric, what are you doing?”

“An essay on King Canute,” Cat said innocently.

Then Mr. Saunders did turn around, but by that time the paper was straight and the pen in Cat’s right hand. “Which hand were you writing with?” he said. Cat was used to this. He held up his right hand with the pen in it. “It looked like both hands to me,” Mr. Saunders said, and he came over and looked at the page Cat had written. “It was both.”

“It doesn’t show,” Cat said miserably.

“Not much,” Mr. Saunders agreed. “Does it amuse you to write with alternate hands, or something?”

“No,” Cat confessed. “But I’m left-handed.”

Then, as Cat had feared, Mr. Saunders flew into a towering rage. His face went red. He slammed his big, knobby hand down on Cat’s desk so that Cat jumped and the inkwell jumped too, sending ink splashing over Mr. Saunders’ great hand and over Cat’s essay. “Left-handed!” he roared. “Then why the Black Gentleman don’t you write with your left hand, boy?”

“They—they punish me if I do,” Cat faltered, very shaken, and very perplexed to find Mr. Saunders was angry for such a peculiar reason.

“Then they deserve to be tied up in knots and roasted!” roared Mr. Saunders, “whoever they are! You’re doing yourself untold harm by obeying them, boy! If I catch you writing with your right hand again, you’ll be in really serious trouble!”

“Yes,” Cat said, relieved but still very shaken. He looked mournfully at his ink-splashed essay and hoped Mr. Saunders might use a little witchcraft on that too. But Mr. Saunders took the book and tore the page right out.

“Now do it again properly!” he said, slapping the book back in front of Cat.

Cat was still writing all over again about Canute when Mary came in with a tray of milk and biscuits and a cup of coffee for Mr. Saunders. And after the milk and biscuits, Mr. Saunders told Cat and Gwendolen they were free till lunch. “Though not because of a good morning’s work,” he said. “Go out and get some fresh air.” As they went out of the schoolroom, he turned to Roger and Julia. “Now we’ll have a little witchcraft,” he said. “And let’s hope you haven’t forgotten all that too.”

Gwendolen stopped in the doorway and looked at him.

“No. Not you,” Mr. Saunders said to her. “I told you.”

Gwendolen whirled around and ran away, through the shabby playroom and down the corridor beyond. Cat ran after her as hard as he could, but he did not catch up with her until they came to a much grander part of the Castle, where a big marble staircase curled away downward and the light came from an elegant dome in the roof.

“This isn’t the right way,” Cat panted.

“Yes it is,” Gwendolen said fiercely. “I’m going to find Chrestomanci. Why should those two fat little fools learn witchcraft and not me? I’ve got twice their gifts. It took two of them just to levitate a jug of cocoa! So I want Chrestomanci.”

By a stroke of good fortune, Chrestomanci was coming along the gallery on the other side of the staircase, behind a curly marble balustrade. He was wearing a fawn-colored suit now, instead of the imperial dressing gown, but he looked, if possible, even more elegant. By the look on his face, his thoughts were miles away. Gwendolen ran around the head of the marble staircase and stood herself in front of him. Chrestomanci blinked, and looked vaguely from her to Cat. “Was one of you wanting me?”

vocabulary.definition: Using one hand and then the other, switching between them.
vocabulary.example: Does it amuse you to write with alternate hands?

vocabulary.definition: A railing supported by balusters, especially on a balcony, bridge, or terrace.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci was behind a curly marble balustrade.

vocabulary.definition: To have earned or be entitled to something because of actions or qualities.
vocabulary.example: Then they deserve to be tied up in knots and roasted!

vocabulary.definition: A rounded vault forming the roof of a building or structure.
vocabulary.example: The light came from an elegant dome in the roof.

vocabulary.definition: Pleasingly graceful and stylish in appearance or manner.
vocabulary.example: He looked even more elegant.

vocabulary.definition: To speak hesitantly or with a loss of confidence.
vocabulary.example: They—they punish me if I do,” Cat faltered.

vocabulary.definition: In a powerful, aggressive, or intense manner.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen said fiercely.

vocabulary.definition: Magnificent and imposing in appearance.
vocabulary.example: They came to a much grander part of the Castle.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to an empire or emperor; grand and majestic.
vocabulary.example: Instead of the imperial dressing gown.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing lack of guilt or wrongdoing.
vocabulary.example: Cat said innocently.

vocabulary.definition: Having knobs or rounded lumps.
vocabulary.example: His big, knobby hand slammed down on Cat’s desk.

vocabulary.definition: Used figuratively to mean complicated or difficult situations, or tied tightly.
vocabulary.example: Deserve to be tied up in knots.

vocabulary.definition: To rise or cause to rise and float in the air, typically by means of supernatural powers.
vocabulary.example: It took two of them just to levitate a jug of cocoa.

vocabulary.definition: A hard crystalline metamorphic form of limestone, typically white and polished for use in sculpture or architecture.
vocabulary.example: A big marble staircase curled away downward.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that expresses sadness or sorrow.
vocabulary.example: He looked mournfully at his ink-splashed essay.

vocabulary.definition: To follow commands, rules, or instructions.
vocabulary.example: You’re doing yourself harm by obeying them.

vocabulary.definition: To breathe quickly and heavily, typically after exertion.
vocabulary.example: Cat panted as he said, 'This isn’t the right way.'

vocabulary.definition: Strange or odd; unusual.
vocabulary.example: He was angry for such a peculiar reason.

vocabulary.definition: Completely baffled; very puzzled.
vocabulary.example: Cat was perplexed to find Mr. Saunders angry for such a peculiar reason.

vocabulary.definition: To shout loudly and forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Left-handed!” he roared.

vocabulary.definition: An unexpected piece of good luck.
vocabulary.example: By a stroke of good fortune, Chrestomanci was coming along.

vocabulary.definition: To remove something by pulling it forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders took the book and tore the page right out.

vocabulary.definition: An intense, very strong feeling of anger.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders flew into a towering rage.

vocabulary.definition: Considerable or incalculable damage or injury.
vocabulary.example: You’re doing yourself untold harm by obeying them.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is uncertain, unclear, or not clearly expressed.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci looked vaguely from her to Cat.
